name: Animalia
description: Animalia is one of the major kingdoms of life, encompassing all multicellular, eukaryotic organisms that obtain energy by consuming other organisms. Animals are defined by their ability to move voluntarily at some stage of life, their lack of a cell wall, and their development from a blastula embryo. The kingdom spans from simple sponges to complex vertebrates and contains an estimated 8–10 million species.

children.name: Mammalia
children.description: Warm-blooded vertebrates with hair or fur. Young are typically born live and nourished with milk produced by the mother. The class includes over 6,400 species ranging from bats and whales to primates and hoofed animals.

children.name: Carnivora
children.description: Despite the name, Carnivora is defined by shared ancestry and skull anatomy, not diet. The order includes dedicated meat-eaters like cats and wolves, but also omnivores like bears and raccoons, and even the bamboo-eating giant panda.

children.name: Cetacea
children.description: Fully aquatic mammals descended from land-dwelling artiodactyls, most closely related to hippos. About 90 living species divided into Mysticeti (baleen whales) and Odontoceti (toothed whales). The blue whale is the largest animal known to have ever existed.

children.name: Perissodactyla
children.description: Hoofed mammals that bear weight on an odd number of toes. Horses walk on a single toe per foot — the hoof is a highly modified toenail. Rhinoceroses and tapirs retain three. Counterintuitively, this makes horses the most "odd-toed" of all.

children.name: Artiodactyla
children.description: Hoofed mammals that bear weight on an even number of toes (2 or 4). The most species-rich ungulate order, including cattle, deer, pigs, camels, and giraffes. Many are ruminants with multi-chambered stomachs for digesting tough plant material.

children.name: Caprinae
children.taxonomicNote: Subfamily of Bovidae (tribe Caprini), treated here as a family-level group for consistency.

children.name: Bovidae
children.taxonomicNote: Bovinae subfamily — cattle, buffalo, and bison. Caprinae (sheep & goats) is treated separately.

children.name: Lagomorpha
children.description: Small herbivorous mammals with continuously growing incisors and a second pair of small peg teeth behind the upper incisors. Unlike rodents, lagomorphs have four upper incisors. They are highly successful colonisers found on every continent except Antarctica.

children.name: Primates
children.description: The order that includes humans and their closest relatives. Divided into Strepsirrhini (wet-nosed primates: lemurs, lorises) and Haplorhini (dry-nosed: tarsiers, monkeys, apes). Primates are characterised by forward-facing eyes, grasping hands, and relatively large brains. Around 500 living species.

children.name: Platyrrhini
children.taxonomicNote: Platyrrhini parvorder — treated here as a family-level group spanning Cebidae, Callitrichidae, Atelidae, and Pitheciidae.

children.name: Diprotodontia
children.description: The largest order of marsupials, containing kangaroos, wallabies, possums, wombats, and koalas. Diprotodontia are united by fused lower incisors (hence the name, 'two front teeth') and a syndactylous second and third toe. With over 140 species, they represent the dominant large land mammals of Australasia.

children.name: Rodentia
children.description: The largest mammal order with around 2,600 species — over 40% of all mammal species. Rodents are defined by a single pair of continuously growing incisors in each jaw, which are self-sharpening through use. They occupy virtually every terrestrial habitat on Earth and range in size from the 7-gram pygmy jerboa to the 66-kg capybara.

children.name: Chiroptera
children.description: Bats — the only mammals capable of true sustained flight. With around 1,400 species they represent roughly 20% of all mammal species and are found on every continent except Antarctica. Most are nocturnal and navigate by echolocation, emitting ultrasonic pulses and analysing the returning echoes to build a detailed acoustic image of their environment. Bats are critical pollinators, seed dispersers, and insect controllers.

children.name: Eulipotyphla
children.description: Hedgehogs, shrews, and moles — small, largely insectivorous mammals that represent some of the most ancient placental lineages. Despite superficial similarities to rodents, eulipotyphlans are not closely related to them. Shrews have the highest metabolic rate of any mammal and must eat almost continuously to survive.

children.name: Proboscidea
children.description: Elephants and their extinct relatives — the largest living land animals. Proboscideans are defined by the proboscis (trunk), a fusion of the nose and upper lip containing over 40,000 muscles. The order was once highly diverse with over 170 genera; today only three species survive.

children.name: Pholidota
children.description: Pangolins — the world's most trafficked mammals. The eight living species are covered in large, overlapping keratin scales (the only scaled mammals) and feed exclusively on ants and termites with a long, sticky tongue. All eight species are threatened with extinction due to illegal hunting.

children.name: Pilosa
children.description: Sloths and anteaters — Neotropical xenarthrans defined by extra articulations in the vertebrae. Pilosa contains the most extreme dietary specialists among mammals: anteaters consume only ants and termites, while sloths subsist almost entirely on leaves, digesting them so slowly that a single meal may take a month to process.

children.name: Cingulata
children.description: Armadillos — the only living mammals with a true bony shell (the carapace), formed from ossified skin plates called osteoderms. The 21 species are entirely restricted to the Americas. The nine-banded armadillo is notable for always giving birth to identical quadruplets from a single fertilised egg.

children.name: Dasyuromorphia
children.description: Carnivorous marsupials of Australasia — the dasyuromorphs fill the ecological niches of small and medium predators across Australia, New Guinea, and nearby islands. The order once included the Thylacine (Tasmanian tiger), the largest marsupial carnivore of modern times, which was driven to extinction in 1936.

children.name: Didelphimorphia
children.description: New World opossums — the only marsupials native to the Americas, and the most primitive living marsupials. With over 120 species they are extraordinarily diverse, ranging from the Virginia opossum of Canada to tiny mouse-sized species in the Amazon. Unlike Australian marsupials, most opossums have a prehensile tail.

children.name: Monotremata
children.description: Monotremes — the most ancient living mammals, unique for laying eggs instead of giving birth to live young. Only five living species survive: the platypus and four species of echidna, all restricted to Australia and New Guinea. They possess a mix of reptilian and mammalian traits including a beak-like snout, venom spurs (in platypus males), and electroreception.

children.name: Aves
children.description: Feathered, warm-blooded vertebrates descended from theropod dinosaurs. All living birds have beaks, lay hard-shelled eggs, and are endothermic. With around 10,000 species they are the most diverse group of land vertebrates.

children.name: Galliformes
children.description: Heavy-bodied, ground-dwelling birds built for short bursts of flight rather than sustained soaring. Galliformes gave us almost all domestic poultry — chickens, turkeys, guinea fowl, and quail — and include the spectacular peafowl and pheasants.

children.name: Anseriformes
children.description: Aquatic birds characterised by webbed feet, broad flat bills, and dense waterproofed plumage. The order includes ducks, geese, and swans, along with the more primitive screamers of South America. Many species undertake long seasonal migrations.

children.name: Piciformes
children.description: Mostly tree-climbing birds with zygodactyl feet (two toes forward, two back) and stiffened tail feathers used as a prop against bark. Woodpeckers drum on resonant dead wood to establish territory and excavate nest cavities that later shelter dozens of other species — they are among the most important keystone cavity-nesters in forest ecosystems.

children.name: Columbiformes
children.description: Plump, round-headed birds with small bills and a rapid, direct flight. Columbiformes are among the most successful bird orders, inhabiting every continent except Antarctica. They feed on seeds and fruit, drink by sucking water rather than tilting the head back, and produce crop milk to feed their young — a trait otherwise found only in flamingos and some penguins.

children.name: Passeriformes
children.description: The largest order of birds, containing over 6,000 species — more than half of all bird species on Earth. Passerines are defined by a specialised foot with three toes pointing forward and one back, locking automatically when the bird perches. The order includes the songbirds, whose complex vocalisations are produced by a uniquely elaborate syrinx.

children.name: Strigiformes
children.description: Nocturnal birds of prey with forward-facing eyes enclosed in a facial disc that acts as a parabolic sound reflector, asymmetrically positioned ears for precise sound triangulation, and silent flight enabled by specialised comb-edged feathers that suppress turbulence. Owls can strike prey in total darkness by sound alone. With around 250 species they occupy virtually every terrestrial habitat on Earth, from Arctic tundra to tropical rainforest.

children.name: Accipitriformes
children.description: Diurnal birds of prey — the hawks, eagles, kites, harriers, and Old World vultures — united by a hooked bill, powerful talons, and keen binocular vision with up to eight times the resolving power of a human eye. Accipitridae is the largest raptor family with around 260 species. The order also includes the Osprey (Pandionidae) and the Secretary Bird (Sagittariidae).

children.name: Charadriiformes
children.description: A highly diverse order of shorebirds, gulls, terns, and auks united by a shared ancestry despite very different appearances and lifestyles. With over 350 species it is one of the largest bird orders. Most are associated with coastal and aquatic habitats and many make extraordinary migrations. Gulls and terns are among the most familiar seabirds of northern Europe, while the waders (sandpipers, plovers, snipe) are the defining birds of estuaries and mudflats.

children.name: Pelecaniformes
children.description: A diverse order of waterbirds that includes herons, egrets, bitterns, pelicans, ibises, and spoonbills. Herons and bitterns (Ardeidae) are the most widespread and familiar group, found on every continent except Antarctica. They are characterised by long legs, long necks, and dagger-like bills used to strike fish and amphibians with lightning speed.

children.name: Falconiformes
children.description: Falcons — swift, agile, pointed-winged birds of prey. DNA analysis revealed they are more closely related to parrots and passerines than to hawks and eagles. The Peregrine is the fastest animal on Earth in a hunting stoop.

children.name: Apodiformes
children.description: An order containing swifts and hummingbirds — birds adapted for extreme aerial life. Swifts have legs so reduced they are incapable of walking; they spend the vast majority of their lives on the wing, sleeping, eating, and even mating in the air.

children.name: Cuculiformes
children.description: Cuckoos and their allies — a cosmopolitan order of over 140 species. Around 40% are obligate brood parasites, laying eggs in the nests of other species. The Common Cuckoo is one of the most famous and celebrated birds of European spring.

children.name: Caprimulgiformes
children.description: Crepuscular and nocturnal birds with wide gapes for catching flying insects at dusk. Cryptically patterned in grey and brown, they are almost invisible when roosting on the ground. Around 100 species worldwide.

children.name: Coraciiformes
children.description: A diverse order of jewel-coloured birds including kingfishers, bee-eaters, and rollers, united by syndactyl feet (fused front toes) and hole-nesting behaviour. Kingfishers are found on every continent except Antarctica.

children.name: Upupiformes
children.description: A small order of just three closely related hoopoe species, characterised by bold black-and-white barred wings, cinnamon plumage, and a dramatic fan-shaped erectile crest. Found across Africa, Europe, and Asia.

children.name: Psittaciformes
children.description: An order of intelligent, highly social birds renowned for their vocal learning ability, strong zygodactyl feet, and curved beaks. Parrots are found across tropical and subtropical regions worldwide, with the greatest diversity in South America and Australasia. Many species are long-lived and form strong pair bonds, and several are among the most endangered bird groups due to habitat loss and the pet trade.

children.name: Sphenisciformes
children.description: Penguins — flightless, highly specialised seabirds of the Southern Hemisphere. Their wings have evolved into flippers for swimming, their bones are dense for diving, and a layer of insulating blubber allows them to survive in the coldest waters on Earth. The 18 living species range from the tropical Galápagos Penguin to the Emperor Penguin, which breeds on Antarctic sea ice in the dead of winter. No penguins occur in the Arctic.

children.name: Phoenicopteriformes
children.description: Flamingos — tall, gregarious wading birds renowned for their striking pink colouration, which comes from carotenoid pigments in the brine shrimp and algae they filter from shallow saline lakes. They are the only birds that feed with their heads upside down. Flamingos are highly social, forming colonies that can number over a million individuals. Their fossil record extends back 30 million years.

children.name: Procellariiformes
children.description: Tubenoses — seabirds defined by their prominent nasal passages (tubenose) that excrete excess salt. This order includes the petrels, shearwaters, storm petrels, and albatrosses. They are masterful long-distance flyers, covering entire ocean basins on their migrations. Many species nest in burrows on remote islands and only return to land under cover of darkness. The order ranges from the tiny 20-gram European Storm Petrel to the wandering albatross with its 3.5-metre wingspan.

children.name: Pterocliformes
children.description: Sandgrouse — medium-sized, ground-dwelling birds of arid and semi-arid regions, placed in their own order. They are pigeon-like in shape but with pointed wings, a short bill, and feathered legs. Their most remarkable adaptation is the ability of males to absorb water in specially modified belly feathers and carry it to chicks at nests up to 30 km from the nearest water. Cryptic plumage renders them nearly invisible against desert gravel.

children.name: Struthioniformes
children.description: Ostriches — the largest living birds. Struthioniformes includes the flightless ratites of Africa. Ostriches are the fastest birds on land, reaching speeds of 70 km/h, and lay the largest eggs of any living bird. Males are striking black-and-white; females are brown.

children.name: Rheiformes
children.description: Rheas — large, flightless birds of South America, resembling ostriches but smaller. Three-toed feet distinguish them from the two-toed ostrich. Males incubate eggs and raise chicks alone, often gathering clutches from multiple females.

children.name: Apterygiformes
children.description: Kiwis — iconic, flightless birds of New Zealand, unique among birds. They have tiny vestigial wings, hair-like feathers, nostrils at the tip of the bill, and lay the largest egg relative to body size of any bird. Nocturnal and highly endangered, with all five species threatened.

children.name: Casuariiformes
children.description: Cassowaries and the Emu — large, flightless birds of Australasia. The Southern Cassowary is often called the world's most dangerous bird for its powerful legs and dagger-like claws. The Emu is Australia's largest bird and can sprint at 50 km/h.

children.name: Tinamiformes
children.description: Tinamous — secretive, ground-dwelling birds of Central and South America. Despite their quail-like appearance, tinamous are the closest living relatives of the giant flightless ratites. Unlike ratites, they can fly, but poorly. Their whistled songs are characteristic sounds of Neotropical forests.

children.name: Musophagiformes
children.description: Turacos — medium-sized, arboreal birds of sub-Saharan Africa. Their plumage contains unique copper-based pigments (turacin and turacoverdin) found in no other bird group. They are weak fliers but agile clamberers through tree canopies. Their loud, raucous calls are a signature sound of African forests.

children.name: Phaethontiformes
children.description: Tropicbirds — elegant, white seabirds of tropical oceans with extremely long central tail feathers. They are pelagic, coming to land only to breed on remote islands. Their ringing calls and graceful flight have made them legendary among sailors.

children.name: Eurypygiformes
children.description: A small order of two remarkable species. The Kagu of New Caledonia has a spectacular crest and powder-down feathers. The Sunbittern of the Americas displays brilliant eyespots on its wings in a threat display. Both exhibit unique features not found in other birds.

children.name: Coliiformes
children.description: Mousebirds — small, gregarious birds of sub-Saharan Africa named for their mousy-grey plumage and habit of scurrying mouse-like through bushes. They have unique feet with reversible outer toes and feed almost entirely on fruit and leaves. The only order endemic entirely to Africa.

children.name: Leptosomiformes
children.description: The cuckoo-roller — a single species placed in its own order, endemic to Madagascar and the Comoros. Its evolutionary relationships have puzzled ornithologists for centuries; DNA now places it closest to the owls and mousebirds.

children.name: Trogoniformes
children.description: Trogons and quetzals — strikingly colourful birds of tropical forests, with iridescent green and red plumage, a distinctive 'trogon' posture (perching upright with tail drooping), and heterodactyl feet (first two toes forward, two back). The Resplendent Quetzal is the national bird of Guatemala.

children.name: Bucerotiformes
children.description: Hornbills, wood hoopoes, and ground hornbills — birds with large, often casqued bills. Hornbills are famous for their nesting behaviour: the female seals herself inside a tree cavity with mud, leaving only a slit for the male to pass food through. Found across Africa and Asia.

children.name: Cariamiformes
children.description: Seriemas — long-legged, predatory birds of South American grasslands. They are the closest living relatives of the extinct terror birds (Phorusrhacidae) that dominated South American ecosystems for 60 million years. Seriemas hunt insects, reptiles, and small mammals, striking with powerful feet.

children.name: Reptilia
children.description: Cold-blooded amniotes with scales or scutes, occupying every continent except Antarctica. Reptilia as a living group encompasses turtles, lizards, snakes, amphisbaenians, tuatara, and crocodilians — though birds are technically reptiles too under modern phylogenetics. Turtles are among the oldest reptile lineages, their body plan essentially unchanged for over 200 million years.

children.name: Testudines
children.description: The only living reptiles with a bony shell — an upper carapace fused to the spine and ribs, and a lower plastron. Turtles cannot crawl out of their shell; it is part of their skeleton. With around 360 species they are found in freshwater, the sea, and on land, and several lineages have evolved independently to live in extreme environments from alpine meadows to open ocean.

children.name: Squamata
children.description: The largest order of reptiles with over 10,000 species — lizards, snakes, and amphisbaenians. Squamates are distinguished by their shed-able, overlapping scales and a unique jaw mechanism (cranial kinesis) that allows independent movement of multiple skull bones. Snakes evolved from lizards, losing their legs gradually; vestigial pelvic girdles persist in pythons and boas. The order contains the world's longest and heaviest reptiles (reticulated python, green anaconda) as well as the smallest (nano-chameleon).

children.name: Crocodylia
children.description: The most behaviourally complex living reptiles and the closest relatives of birds. Their lineage survived the mass extinction 66 million years ago. Despite a reputation as mindless predators, crocodilians show complex parental care and social communication.

children.name: Rhynchocephalia
children.description: An ancient order once spanning the globe, now reduced to a single surviving species on offshore islands of New Zealand. The tuatara diverged from lizards 240 million years ago and has barely changed since.

children.name: Chondrichthyes
children.description: Fish with skeletons made entirely of cartilage rather than bone. The class includes sharks, rays, and chimaeras — over 1,100 species. Their evolutionary lineage stretches back more than 450 million years, making them among the oldest jawed vertebrates.

children.name: Carcharhiniformes
children.description: The largest order of sharks with around 270 species. Ground sharks are characterised by a nictitating membrane over the eye, two dorsal fins without spines, an anal fin, and five gill slits. The order includes many of the most familiar and ecologically important species.

children.name: Lamniformes
children.description: Fifteen species of powerful, largely pelagic sharks including the great white, makos, and basking shark. Lamniformes are distinguished by their large mouths extending behind the eyes and lack of a nictitating membrane. Several species are endothermic, maintaining body temperature above the surrounding water.

children.name: Orectolobiformes
children.description: Around 45 species of bottom-dwelling and reef-associated sharks, plus the whale shark — the largest fish on Earth. Carpet sharks are typically flattened with cryptic patterning, though the filter-feeding whale shark and nurse sharks are notable exceptions. Most are confined to the Indo-Pacific.

children.name: Myliobatiformes
children.description: Stingrays and their relatives — over 200 species of flattened cartilaginous fish with enlarged pectoral fins fused to the head. Most have one or more barbed venomous spines on the tail. The group includes the giant manta ray, the largest ray with a wingspan exceeding 7 metres.

children.name: Amphibia
children.description: Cold-blooded vertebrates that metamorphose from water-breathing larvae to air-breathing adults. Amphibians are the first vertebrates to have colonised land, yet most remain tied to fresh water for reproduction. With around 8,000 species they are the most threatened vertebrate class — around 41% of species are at risk of extinction, primarily due to the amphibian chytrid fungus *Batrachochytrium dendrobatidis*, habitat destruction, and climate change.

children.name: Urodela
children.description: Around 740 species of tailed amphibians — the salamanders, newts, and sirens — characterised by a lizard-like body plan retained through life. Most breathe through lungs as adults, though some (hellbenders, olms) are permanently aquatic and breathe primarily through skin. Salamanders are among the few vertebrates capable of true limb regeneration, regrowing lost limbs, tails, eyes, and even portions of the heart.

children.name: Anura
children.description: The largest amphibian order with around 7,000 species. Frogs are defined by their tail-less adult body, long hind limbs, and ability to leap. They inhabit every continent except Antarctica and every environment from tropical rainforest to desert and high alpine meadow. The order includes the most endangered amphibians on Earth: more than 100 species are thought to have gone extinct since the 1970s due to chytridiomycosis.

children.name: Gymnophiona
children.description: Legless, burrowing amphibians of the tropics — the least-known of the three amphibian orders. With over 200 species they occupy every tropical continent; most spend their lives underground, navigating by chemoreception through a pair of sensory tentacles unique among vertebrates.

children.name: Actinopterygii
children.description: The largest class of vertebrates with over 30,000 species — nearly half of all vertebrate species on Earth. Ray-finned fish are defined by fins supported by bony spines (rays) rather than the fleshy lobed fins of lobe-finned fish. They range from the tiny 8-mm Paedocypris to the 3-metre Atlantic bluefin tuna, and occupy every aquatic habitat from deep ocean trenches to high-altitude glacial lakes. In Sweden, ray-finned fish dominate both marine and freshwater ecosystems.

children.name: Clupeiformes
children.description: Herrings, sardines, shad, and anchovies — small, schooling, silvery fish that form some of the largest fish populations on Earth. They occupy a critical position in marine food webs: filter-feeding on plankton and in turn being consumed by cod, salmon, tuna, seabirds, and whales. The Baltic herring (strömming) is the most important commercial fish in the Baltic Sea and the cornerstone of Swedish fish culture.

children.name: Anguilliformes
children.description: Eels — elongated, snake-like fish with a catadromous life cycle: they spawn at sea, migrate as larvae to freshwater or estuarine habitats, grow to adulthood, then return to the open ocean to reproduce and die. The European eel's migration to the Sargasso Sea remains one of the most remarkable animal migrations on Earth. Many eel populations have collapsed due to overfishing, habitat loss, and climate change.

children.name: Salmoniformes
children.description: Salmon and their relatives — cold-water fish of the Northern Hemisphere, distinguished by an adipose fin (a small fleshy fin between the dorsal fin and tail). Many are anadromous, born in rivers, growing to maturity at sea, and returning to their natal rivers to spawn. They are among the most ecologically important and culturally significant fish in the world.

children.name: Esociformes
children.description: Pike and mudminnows — freshwater predators of cold, clear northern lakes and rivers. Pike are the apex predators of European and North American freshwater ecosystems, sitting atop food webs that can include dozens of prey species. Their elongated, torpedo-shaped bodies and ambush hunting strategy make them extraordinarily effective predators.

children.name: Perciformes
children.description: Perch and their relatives — one of the most species-rich fish orders, including many of the most familiar freshwater and marine fish. Perciformes are typically characterised by spiny-rayed fins and a generalised body plan that has proven extraordinarily successful across a range of habitats from Arctic lakes to tropical reefs.

children.name: Gadiformes
children.description: Cod and their relatives — demersal fish of cold northern seas that have shaped human history through their extraordinary abundance and the exploitation that followed. The family Gadidae includes some of the most important food fish in the world. Baltic and North Sea cod stocks are ecologically critical but severely depleted.

children.name: Scorpaeniformes
children.description: Sculpins and their relatives — a diverse order of mostly cold-water fish characterised by a bony plate (suborbital stay) across the cheek. The order includes sculpins, sea ravens, and cabezons, many of which are bottom-dwelling ambush predators with broad flattened heads and large pectoral fins. They are abundant in Arctic, North Atlantic, and North Pacific waters, and Cottus species (freshwater bullheads) are common across Europe and North America.

children.name: Pleuronectiformes
children.description: Flatfish — a distinctive order of asymmetric, bottom-dwelling fish that lie on one side of the body (left or right depending on the species) with both eyes on the upper side. Flatfish begin life as symmetrical larvae that swim upright, then undergo a metamorphosis in which one eye migrates to the other side and the body flattens. They range from the shallow coastal waters of the Baltic to the deep ocean floor, and include some of the most commercially important food fish in Europe: plaice, flounder, sole, dab, and halibut.

children.name: Cypriniformes
children.description: The largest order of freshwater fish with over 4,300 species, including carp, roach, bream, chub, ide, and thousands of tropical species. Cypriniformes lack true teeth in the jaw but have pharyngeal teeth (in the throat) used to crush food. They are the dominant freshwater fish in Europe and Asia by species richness and abundance, forming the base of freshwater food webs.

children.name: Cyprinodontiformes
children.description: Cyprinodontiformes — a diverse order of mostly small freshwater fish, many of which are annual species adapted to temporary water bodies. They include killifishes, guppies, mollies, swordtails, and the famous pupfish of Death Valley. Many species are popular in the aquarium trade for their bright colours and fascinating reproductive strategies.

children.name: Syngnathiformes
children.description: An order of distinctive fish with tubular snouts, fused jaws, and bodies armoured with bony plates rather than scales. Males of the family Syngnathidae (seahorses and pipefish) are unique in the animal kingdom for male pregnancy — the female deposits eggs into the male's brood pouch, where he fertilises and carries them until birth.

children.name: Tardigrada
children.description: Microscopic eight-legged animals found in every habitat on Earth — from deep ocean trenches to Himalayan glaciers, from Antarctic ice to city roof gutters. Tardigrades are the most resilient animals known: in a desiccated state called cryptobiosis they can withstand temperatures approaching absolute zero, pressures six times that of the deepest ocean, ionising radiation 1,000 times the lethal dose for a human, and the complete vacuum of outer space. Around 1,300 species are described, most under half a millimetre long.

children.name: Arthropoda
children.description: The most species-rich phylum on Earth — over one million described species, representing roughly 80% of all known animal species. Arthropods are defined by a chitinous exoskeleton, a segmented body, and jointed appendages. The phylum includes insects, arachnids, crustaceans, and myriapods. Insects alone account for the majority of all described animal species, with conservative estimates placing the total insect species count at 5.5 million.

children.name: Insecta
children.description: The largest class of animals on Earth — over one million described species — with six legs, three body segments (head, thorax, abdomen), and typically two pairs of wings in the adult. Insects have colonised virtually every terrestrial and freshwater habitat. They are the primary pollinators of flowering plants, decomposers of organic matter, and the base of most terrestrial food webs. Insect biomass is declining at alarming rates — estimated losses of 75% in some European regions over 30 years.

children.name: Hymenoptera
children.description: One of the largest insect orders, with over 150,000 described species. Hymenoptera includes the social insects par excellence — honey bees, bumblebees, paper wasps, and ants — as well as thousands of solitary species. Eusociality, where workers forego reproduction to aid the queen, evolved multiple times in this order. The order is distinguished by two pairs of membranous wings linked by hooks, and a narrow 'wasp waist' (petiole) connecting thorax and abdomen.

children.name: Lepidoptera
children.description: The second-largest insect order — over 180,000 described species — distinguished by the covering of tiny scales on their wings (the name means 'scale-winged'). Lepidoptera undergoes complete metamorphosis: egg, larva (caterpillar), pupa (chrysalis), and adult image. They are the most important group of pollinators for wild flowers, and the caterpillars are a critical food source for birds. In Sweden alone, over 2,000 species of butterflies and moths have been recorded, with the Apollo butterfly and the Old World Swallowtail among the most iconic.

children.name: Odonata
children.description: Odonata — ancient aerial predators with over 6,000 described species. They have four independent wings, enormous compound eyes covering most of the head, and an aquatic larval stage (nymph) that is itself a ferocious predator. Damselflies (Zygoptera) hold their wings folded above the body at rest; dragonflies (Anisoptera) hold them open flat.

children.name: Coleoptera
children.description: The largest order in the animal kingdom with over 400,000 described species — roughly one in four of all known species. Beetles are characterised by hardened forewings (elytra) that cover the membranous hindwings. They occupy virtually every ecological niche and include herbivores, predators, scavengers, and decomposers.

children.name: Arachnida
children.description: The second largest class of arthropods with over 110,000 described species, including spiders, scorpions, mites, ticks, and harvestmen. All arachnids have eight legs, no antennae, and chelicerae instead of mandibles. Scorpions are among the oldest terrestrial animals (Silurian, ~430 Mya); all fluoresce bright blue-green under UV light.

children.name: Araneae
children.description: With around 50,000 described species, the most species-rich order of arachnids and the seventh most diverse animal order. All produce silk; virtually all are venomous. Occupy every terrestrial ecosystem except the poles and consume an estimated 400-800 million tonnes of prey annually.

children.name: Scorpiones
children.description: Around 2,500 species with distinctive pedipalp claws and segmented metasoma ending in a venomous stinger. Among the oldest terrestrial animals (Silurian fossils ~430 Mya nearly identical to living forms). All species venomous; around 50-75 pose serious risk to humans. All fluoresce bright blue-green under UV.

children.name: Echinodermata
children.description: Marine invertebrates with a hard spiny calcareous endoskeleton and pentaradial symmetry. The phylum includes starfish, sea urchins, sand dollars, sea cucumbers, and crinoids. Echinoderms are exclusively marine and are found at every ocean depth, from the intertidal zone to the abyssal plain. Most are benthic and slow-moving. The group is characterised by a water vascular system used for locomotion and feeding. With around 7,000 living species they represent the largest phylum with no freshwater or terrestrial representatives.

children.name: Asteroidea
children.description: Starfish — marine stars with five or more arms radiating from a central disc. Around 1,900 species inhabit every ocean. Most are carnivorous, feeding on molluscs and other invertebrates by everting their stomach through the mouth. Many species can regenerate lost arms, and some can regenerate an entirely new individual from a single severed arm.

children.name: Forcipulatida
children.description: An order of starfish characterised by distinctive forked pedicellariae — tiny pincer-like structures on the body surface that keep the surface clear of debris and larvae. Includes the common starfish Asterias rubens, one of the most familiar echinoderms of European waters.

children.name: Echinoidea
children.description: Globular or flattened echinoderms enclosed in a hard calcareous test covered in moveable spines. Around 1,000 species occupy all ocean depths. Sea urchins are important grazers of algae; their population explosions can devastate kelp forests. Sand dollars are flattened, burrowing urchins with greatly reduced spines.

children.name: Camarodonta
children.description: The most diverse order of sea urchins, characterised by a complex jaw apparatus (Aristotle's lantern) with five tooth-bearing plates. Includes the common edible sea urchin and most familiar shallow-water species.

children.name: Holothuroidea
children.description: Elongated, soft-bodied echinoderms with a leathery skin and reduced endoskeleton — the only echinoderms that feel fleshy rather than spiny. Around 1,700 species crawl across the seafloor worldwide, ingesting sediment and extracting organic matter. Many expel sticky threads (Cuvierian tubules) as defence; some eject internal organs and regenerate them.

children.name: Holothuriida
children.description: The largest order of sea cucumbers, containing over 1,000 species characterised by the presence of respiratory trees and a distinctive calcareous ring around the pharynx. Many species are commercially harvested as trepang (bêche-de-mer) for Asian markets.

children.name: Mollusca
children.description: Molluscs — one of the largest animal phyla, including snails, clams, squid, octopuses, and thousands of other species. Molluscs share a soft unsegmented body, often protected by a calcium carbonate shell. They range from microscopic sea butterflies to the colossal squid, the largest invertebrate on Earth. With over 70,000 described species they are second only to arthropods in biodiversity.

children.name: Cephalopoda
children.description: Cephalopods — octopuses, squid, cuttlefish, and nautiluses. The most intelligent invertebrates, with complex brains, camera-like eyes, and jet propulsion. Many can change colour and texture in milliseconds. The class includes the largest invertebrates ever known, with the colossal squid reaching 14 metres.

children.name: Octopoda
children.description: Octopuses — eight-armed cephalopods with no shell, renowned for intelligence, camouflage, and problem-solving ability. Over 300 species inhabit every ocean from coral reefs to the abyssal depths.

children.name: Teuthida
children.description: Squid — fast-swimming cephalopods with eight arms, two longer feeding tentacles, and a streamlined mantle with fins. They are among the most abundant marine predators and include the colossal and giant squid — the largest invertebrates on Earth.

children.name: Sepiida
children.description: Cuttlefish — masters of camouflage with the largest brain-to-body ratio of any invertebrate. Their unique W-shaped pupils and rapidly shifting chromatophore skin patterns make them among the most visually striking marine animals. The internal cuttlebone serves as a buoyancy organ.

children.name: Nautilida
children.description: Nautiluses — living fossils, the last surviving members of an ancient cephalopod lineage that dominated the seas 500 million years ago. Unlike other cephalopods, they have a fully developed external shell divided into chambers, and lack colour-changing skin.

children.name: Vampyromorphida
children.description: A single species occupying a deep-sea twilight zone between 600 and 900 metres. Despite its name, the vampire squid is not a true squid or octopus — it is the sole surviving member of an ancient order. It has the largest eyes relative to body size of any animal.

children.name: Spirulida
children.description: A single deep-sea species with an internal coiled shell that resembles a ram's horn. It is the only cephalopod with a truly chambered internal shell, bridging the gap between nautiluses and other coleoids.
